Course Title: AI in Donor Targeting and Fundraising
Category: Education & Public Sector: Education, Government Services, Non-Profit organizations
Description: Ethical risks in profiling and communication strategies.
Prerequisites: Learners should have a basic familiarity with fundraising or donor engagement principles and a general awareness of how digital tools are used in the non-profit or public sector, though no prior experience with artificial intelligence is required.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key ways artificial intelligence is used in donor targeting and fundraising, and explain the ethical risks associated with profiling supporters and personalising communications. You will also be able to apply practical strategies to ensure your organisation uses AI responsibly, balancing effective fundraising goals with transparency, fairness, and respect for donor privacy.

lessons.lesson title: AI in Donor Targeting and Fundraising
lessons.Description: This lesson introduces fundraising professionals to the ways artificial intelligence is being used to identify, segment, and engage potential donors. You will explore how AI tools can analyse data to predict donor behaviour and personalise outreach, while also examining the ethical risks that come with profiling individuals and automating communication strategies. The lesson takes a practical approach, helping you to understand where AI can genuinely support your fundraising efforts and where caution is needed. By the end, you will have a clearer sense of how to use these tools responsibly, in a way that respects donor privacy and maintains the trust your organisation has worked hard to build.
lessons.content: Ethical risks in profiling and communication strategies.

slides.subtitle: AI's Growing Role in Modern Fundraising
slides.slidecontent:
• AI transforms how nonprofits identify and engage donors
• Powerful tools require ethical responsibility
• Balance effectiveness with transparency
• Practical guidance for responsible implementation
• Accessible to all experience levels

slides.subtitle: The Technology Behind Targeted Fundraising
slides.slidecontent:
• Analyses age, income, location, and giving history
• Reveals sensitive personal circumstances
• Operates at scale across entire databases
• Often works without donor awareness
• Can predict behaviour and preferences
• Builds detailed individual portraits

slides.subtitle: The Trust and Privacy Risks
slides.slidecontent:
• Donors unaware of data analysis depth
• Trust erodes when profiling feels intrusive
• GDPR and privacy laws apply strictly
• Consent often unclear or absent
• Vulnerable targeting raises serious concerns
• Reputation damage from perceived misuse

slides.subtitle: Ethical Boundaries in Donor Outreach
slides.slidecontent:
• AI identifies donors experiencing bereavement
• Financial stress patterns trigger targeting
• Health conditions revealed through behaviour
• Religious beliefs inferred from data
• Emotional manipulation risks increase
• Clear policies needed for sensitive situations

slides.subtitle: When AI Perpetuates Historical Inequalities
slides.slidecontent:
• Historical data contains embedded bias
• Certain communities systematically overlooked
• Racial and socioeconomic exclusion patterns repeat
• Geographic blind spots limit reach
• Demographic gaps create unfair outcomes
• Regular auditing catches bias early

slides.subtitle: Practical Steps to Identify AI Bias
slides.slidecontent:
• Review who gets targeted and who doesn't
• Compare AI outputs to population demographics
• Question why certain groups are excluded
• Diverse review teams spot blind spots
• Monitor engagement patterns across communities
• Test assumptions with real-world data

slides.subtitle: Building Ethical AI into Daily Operations
slides.slidecontent:
• Audit data sources for bias regularly
• Implement opt-in consent systems
• Test communications with diverse groups
• Keep humans involved in final decisions
• Document AI processes for accountability
• Train staff to spot problematic outputs

slides.subtitle: Implementing Responsible AI Practices
slides.slidecontent:
• Assess current fundraising processes for AI opportunities
• Establish clear consent and transparency policies
• Build regular bias audits into workflows
• Train team on ethical AI principles
• Start small with pilot programs
• Balance efficiency gains with donor dignity

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on AI in donor targeting and fundraising. AI is no longer just for tech giants - it's increasingly shaping how charities and nonprofits find, understand, and connect with supporters. These tools offer remarkable potential to enhance your fundraising effectiveness, but they also bring significant ethical responsibilities. Throughout this course, we'll explore how to harness AI's power while maintaining the trust and dignity that ethical fundraising requires. Whether you're new to AI or exploring its fundraising applications, this course will give you practical guidance for responsible implementation. Let's begin by understanding what AI can do and where the ethical challenges lie.

Let's start by understanding what AI profiling actually involves. Modern AI systems can analyse vast amounts of data about your donors - their age, income, location, giving history, even their online behaviour patterns. These systems are remarkably sophisticated, able to build detailed individual portraits that predict everything from donation timing to preferred communication channels. They work by finding patterns across thousands or millions of data points, creating profiles that can reveal intimate details about someone's life circumstances, financial situation, or personal values. The technology operates continuously and at scale, processing entire donor databases to identify prospects and personalise outreach. This capability is what makes AI so powerful for fundraising - but it's also where the ethical questions begin.

Here's where things get complicated from an ethical standpoint. Most donors have no idea their data is being analysed at this level of detail. They may have agreed to receive communications, but they likely never expected their personal information would be used to build comprehensive behavioural profiles. When people discover this is happening, trust can evaporate quickly - and rightfully so. The regulatory landscape adds another layer of complexity. GDPR and similar privacy laws place strict obligations on how organisations handle personal data, and these rules apply fully to AI-driven processes. The legal requirement for clear, informed consent becomes particularly challenging when donors don't understand what they're consenting to. This isn't just about compliance - it's about maintaining the fundamental trust that makes fundraising relationships possible.

One of the most troubling aspects of AI profiling is its ability to identify and target vulnerable individuals. These systems can detect patterns that suggest someone is grieving, facing financial hardship, dealing with health issues, or going through other difficult circumstances. While this information might seem useful for crafting empathetic communications, targeting people based on vulnerability crosses serious ethical lines. It raises questions about exploitation and manipulation that go to the heart of ethical fundraising practice. Organizations need clear policies about what types of targeting are acceptable and what crosses the line into predatory behaviour. The power to identify vulnerability comes with the responsibility to protect it, not exploit it for fundraising gain.

One of the most significant risks with AI is that it can perpetuate and amplify existing inequalities. These systems learn from historical data, and if that data contains bias - which it almost certainly does - the AI will reproduce those same patterns. If your organization historically had limited success engaging certain racial, socioeconomic, or geographic communities, your AI model may simply continue to overlook them. This creates a problematic cycle where underrepresented groups remain excluded while overrepresented groups receive excessive attention. The result isn't just ethically concerning - it also limits your fundraising potential by systematically missing entire segments of potential supporters. This kind of bias can be particularly insidious because it often appears as objective, data-driven decision making.

So how do you actually spot bias in your AI systems? It requires active effort and systematic review. Start by regularly examining who your AI is targeting and who it's not. Compare your targeting demographics to the broader population you're trying to reach. Ask hard questions about why certain communities might be underrepresented in your outreach. Diverse review teams are crucial here - people from different backgrounds are more likely to notice when something looks off. Monitor engagement patterns across different demographic groups to see if your AI is creating uneven outcomes. The goal isn't to achieve perfect demographic representation immediately, but to understand where your AI might have blind spots and take steps to address them systematically.

Moving from identifying problems to solving them requires concrete operational changes. Regular auditing of your data sources helps catch bias before it affects your outreach. Implementing clear opt-in consent systems ensures donors understand how their information will be used. Testing communications with diverse focus groups before full deployment can catch problematic messaging early. Perhaps most importantly, maintain human oversight in final targeting decisions - AI should inform your choices, not make them automatically. Document your AI processes clearly so they can be reviewed, questioned, and improved over time. And invest in training your team to recognise when AI outputs seem unfair, inaccurate, or ethically questionable.

As we wrap up, let's focus on practical next steps for your organization. Start by selecting one current fundraising process and evaluate where AI might add genuine value without compromising your ethical standards. Establish clear policies around consent, transparency, and vulnerable targeting before you begin implementation. Build regular bias audits into your standard workflows rather than treating them as one-time exercises. Invest in training your team on both the technical and ethical aspects of AI use. Consider starting with small pilot programs that let you test and refine your approach. Remember, the goal isn't to use AI everywhere, but to use it thoughtfully where it genuinely serves both your mission and your donors' dignity. The most effective AI implementation is one that strengthens rather than undermines the trust your organization has worked to build.

Who is this for: Development Officers, Fundraising Managers, Donor Relations Specialists, Development Directors, Marketing Coordinators, Communications Managers, Non-profit Program Managers
Team or Dept this is for: Development, Fundraising, Marketing, Communications, Non-profit Operations
